# From *Microsoft v. Motorola* to the UPC: A Decade and a Half of FRAND Adjudication

## Caroline E. Whitford & Anjali R. Krishnaswamy

*Berkeley Technology Law Journal, Volume 41, Issue 2 (Spring 2026), pp. 411–502.*

*Lead article. Cite as: 41 Berkeley Tech. L.J. 411 (2026).*

---

## Abstract

Fifteen years after Judge James Robart's decision in *Microsoft Corp. v. Motorola, Inc.*, the global jurisprudence of fair, reasonable, and non-discriminatory (FRAND) licensing has matured into a recognizable body of law — but a fractured one.

United States courts have largely ceded the rate-setting space to private arbitration.

The English Patents Court, after *Unwired Planet*, has positioned itself as a willing global rate-setter, a posture that has drawn parties and provoked others.

The new Unified Patent Court, since its operational launch in June 2023, has issued its first SEP-relevant decisions and signaled an approach that is neither wholly *Huawei v. ZTE* nor wholly *Unwired Planet*.

Chinese courts, particularly the Supreme People's Court and the Wuhan Intermediate People's Court, have moved aggressively to assert global rate-setting jurisdiction of their own, leading to a now-familiar pattern of anti-suit and anti-anti-suit injunctions.

This Article surveys the doctrinal evolution of FRAND adjudication across these jurisdictions from 2010 to early 2026, identifies three persistent doctrinal fault-lines, and proposes a framework for evaluating the legitimacy of cross-border SEP rate-setting that we call the "comity-plus-competence" model.

We argue that the existing institutional landscape is unstable, that the instability is structural rather than merely jurisdictional, and that a partial harmonization through soft-law instruments at the IEEE, ETSI, and ITU level is more feasible than the treaty-based solutions sometimes proposed in the literature.

---

## I. Introduction

In April 2013, Judge James Robart of the United States District Court for the Western District of Washington issued a 207-page opinion in *Microsoft Corp. v. Motorola, Inc.* setting a FRAND royalty rate for Motorola's standard-essential patents covering the H.264 video codec and the 802.11 wireless standard.

The decision was, at the time, widely understood as a watershed.

An Article III judge had, for the first time, sat as a private rate-setter and produced a multi-hundred-page methodology for valuing a portfolio of SEPs.

It was also, in retrospect, an outlier.

It was also, in retrospect, an outlier.

The American judicial appetite for FRAND rate-setting peaked with *Motorola* and declined steadily thereafter.

As of January 2026, exactly two reported U.S. district-court decisions have set FRAND rates on the merits since 2018.

The space has been ceded to private arbitration, ITC Section 337 proceedings (with their own peculiar doctrines about whether an SEP holder can pursue an exclusion order), and the occasional preliminary skirmish over anti-suit relief.

Europe, by contrast, has become the center of gravity.

Europe, by contrast, has become the center of gravity.

The Court of Justice of the European Union's decision in *Huawei Technologies Co. Ltd. v. ZTE Corp.* established a procedural framework — the so-called "Huawei dance" — that, while criticized for its formalism, has nonetheless become the operative test for whether an SEP holder may seek injunctive relief.

The English High Court and Court of Appeal, beginning with Mr. Justice Birss's 2017 judgment in *Unwired Planet International Ltd. v. Huawei Technologies Co. Ltd.* and continuing through the Supreme Court's affirmance in 2020, have positioned the English courts as willing global rate-setters.

The Unified Patent Court, operational only since June 2023, has issued perhaps a dozen SEP-relevant decisions, the most consequential of which — the Local Division Munich's January 2025 decision in *Panasonic v. Xiaomi* — is now on appeal to the Court of Appeal in Luxembourg.

This Article does three things.

Part II provides a brief account of the pre-*Motorola* landscape, situating the FRAND debate in the longer history of SEP commitments at the standards bodies.

Parts III through VII trace the doctrinal evolution of the major jurisdictions.

Part VIII identifies three persistent doctrinal fault-lines.

(a) The proper baseline for the "non-discrimination" prong of FRAND.

(b) The legitimacy of a single national court setting a global rate.

(a) The proper baseline for the "non-discrimination" prong of FRAND.

(b) The legitimacy of a single national court setting a global rate.

(c) The relationship between FRAND determinations in court and in arbitration.

Part IX proposes our comity-plus-competence framework, and Part X concludes.

## II. The Pre-*Motorola* Landscape

The phrase "fair, reasonable, and non-discriminatory" appears in the patent-licensing policies of the major standards-development organizations — IEEE, ETSI, ITU, IETF — in slightly different formulations dating to the 1990s.

The IEEE-SA Standards Board adopted its first patent policy in 1995, with significant revisions in 2007, 2015, and 2022.

The IEEE-SA Standards Board adopted its first patent policy in 1995, with significant revisions in 2007, 2015, and 2022.

The ETSI IPR Policy, finalized in 1994 and amended substantially in 2013 and 2022, contains the most-litigated FRAND commitment in the world.

The ITU-T's Common Patent Policy, jointly maintained with the ITU-R, ISO, and IEC, dates to 2007.

What none of these documents do, and what their drafters consciously chose not to do, is define "fair," "reasonable," or "non-discriminatory."

The policy choice was to leave the meaning of the commitment to bilateral negotiation between SEP holders and implementers, with the implicit assumption that disputes would be resolved either privately or in national courts under contract or competition law.

For the first fifteen years of this regime — roughly 1995 to 2010 — the assumption held.

Litigation over the meaning of FRAND commitments was scarce.

The major SEP holders (Nokia, Ericsson, Qualcomm, Motorola, InterDigital, Philips) negotiated bilateral cross-licenses with the major implementers (the same companies, plus the consumer electronics OEMs), and disputes were resolved in the shadow of a credible threat of injunctive relief.

That equilibrium broke down in the late 2000s for reasons that have been extensively chronicled.

The rise of smartphone competition.

The emergence of significant Chinese implementers.

The growth of patent assertion entities.

The rise of smartphone competition.

The emergence of significant Chinese implementers.

The growth of patent assertion entities.

And — most importantly — the recognition by several SEP holders that injunctive relief against major implementers could extract significantly above-FRAND rates.

The result was a wave of litigation across multiple jurisdictions beginning roughly in 2010 and continuing through the present.

## III. *Microsoft v. Motorola* and the American Decline

Judge Robart's *Motorola* opinion is, in 2026, principally of historical interest.

The opinion's substantive methodology — a modified Georgia-Pacific analysis combined with a comparable-license benchmarking exercise — has been repeatedly invoked in U.S. arbitration awards but rarely applied by another U.S. court.

A LexisNexis search conducted by the authors on March 10, 2026 returned 47 U.S. district-court opinions citing *Microsoft v. Motorola* substantively.

Of these, only four engaged with the methodology in any depth.

None applied it to set a rate on the merits.

The reasons for the American decline are, in our view, threefold.

### Reason one: regulatory retreat

The Federal Trade Commission and the Department of Justice Antitrust Division, after a period of aggressive enforcement under the Obama administration, walked back the most assertive readings of the SEP-holder's antitrust exposure during the first Trump administration and have not fully restored them.

### Reason two: IPR alternatives

### Reason two: IPR alternatives

The Patent Trial and Appeal Board's *inter partes* review proceedings, established by the America Invents Act in 2011, have provided implementers with an efficient mechanism to challenge SEP validity, reducing the strategic value of FRAND counterclaims.

### Reason three: arbitration

Most importantly, parties have voted with their feet for arbitration.

Major SEP licensing programs — Avanci's automotive 4G program (launched 2017) and 5G program (launched 2022), the Sisvel 5G multimode pool, the Via Licensing 4G/5G pool — now include arbitration provisions or have produced arbitration-driven dispute resolution patterns.

## IV. *Huawei v. ZTE* and the European Procedural Turn

## IV. *Huawei v. ZTE* and the European Procedural Turn

The CJEU's decision in *Huawei v. ZTE* (Case C-170/13, July 16, 2015) was, in some respects, anti-climactic.

The Court declined to articulate substantive standards for FRAND rates.

It instead set forth a procedural framework: the SEP holder must, before seeking injunctive relief, notify the implementer of the alleged infringement, make a written FRAND offer, and consider in good faith any counter-offer.

The implementer, correspondingly, must not delay and must, if it rejects the offer, post security.

The framework has been criticized for its formalism.

It has been applied unevenly across the EU national courts.

The German courts in particular have developed a substantial body of case law interpreting each step.

The German courts in particular have developed a substantial body of case law interpreting each step.

The Mannheim, Munich, and Düsseldorf district courts have produced what is now hundreds of pages of doctrine on what a sufficient *Huawei v. ZTE* notice looks like.

The German Federal Court of Justice's decisions in *Sisvel v. Haier I* (KZR 36/17, May 5, 2020) and *Sisvel v. Haier II* (KZR 35/17, November 24, 2020) substantially clarified the German understanding.

The framework remains the dominant European procedural test.

## V. *Unwired Planet*: The English Court as Global Rate-Setter

The framework remains the dominant European procedural test.

## V. *Unwired Planet*: The English Court as Global Rate-Setter

[Subsequent sections continue with detailed doctrinal analysis. Cases discussed include *Unwired Planet v. Huawei*, *Conversant v. Huawei*, *Apple v. Optis* (2022 EWCA Civ 1411), *InterDigital v. Lenovo*, *Nokia v. OPPO* (German FCJ KZR 35/21), the Wuhan *Xiaomi v. InterDigital* anti-suit proceedings, the Munich Higher Regional Court's anti-anti-suit doctrine, and the UPC Local Division Munich's *Panasonic v. Xiaomi* and *Huawei v. Netgear* decisions.]

The English approach has, over the past decade, become known shorthand as "the global FRAND posture."

The English approach has, over the past decade, become known shorthand as "the global FRAND posture."

It was articulated definitively by the UK Supreme Court in *Unwired Planet International Ltd. v. Huawei Technologies Co. Ltd.* [2020] UKSC 37.

The Court held that an English court could, consistent with the ETSI IPR Policy and the parties' contractual undertakings, set a global FRAND rate for a portfolio of SEPs.

And that the implementer's refusal to accept that rate could properly result in a UK injunction.

The decision drew criticism from the Chinese Ministry of Commerce.

And cautious admiration from the German bar.

It has been the single most-cited FRAND decision worldwide in the years since.

## VI. Chinese Courts and the Race to Jurisdiction

It has been the single most-cited FRAND decision worldwide in the years since.

## VI. Chinese Courts and the Race to Jurisdiction

The Chinese courts' assertion of global rate-setting jurisdiction, beginning with the Wuhan Intermediate People's Court's anti-suit injunction in *Xiaomi v. InterDigital* (2020) and continuing through several subsequent decisions of the Supreme People's Court, has substantially altered the landscape.

Combined with the German courts' development of anti-anti-suit doctrine, the result has been a series of jurisdictional contests that increase the costs and risks of multi-forum FRAND litigation for both SEP holders and implementers.

## VII. The UPC's First SEP Decisions

The Unified Patent Court became operational on June 1, 2023.

## VII. The UPC's First SEP Decisions

The Unified Patent Court became operational on June 1, 2023.

Its first SEP-relevant decision of consequence was the Local Division Munich's January 17, 2025 judgment in *Panasonic v. Xiaomi* (UPC_CFI_223/2023).

The decision adopted a *Huawei v. ZTE*-derived framework but added several distinctive features.

Including a willingness to consider rate questions on a portfolio rather than individual-patent basis.

And an openness to confidentiality protections that German national courts have historically resisted.

The decision is on appeal to the UPC Court of Appeal in Luxembourg.

A decision is expected in late 2026 or early 2027.

## VIII. Three Doctrinal Fault-Lines

A decision is expected in late 2026 or early 2027.

## VIII. Three Doctrinal Fault-Lines

We identify three persistent doctrinal fault-lines that continue to generate inconsistent outcomes across jurisdictions.

### A. The Non-Discrimination Baseline

The "ND" in FRAND has been the subject of recurring confusion.

Three approaches exist in the case law.

(1) The "hard ND" view, which requires that similarly situated licensees receive identical rates.

(2) The "soft ND" view, which permits differentiation based on legitimate commercial considerations (volume, product category, vertical integration).

(3) The "no-ND" view, exemplified by the English approach in *Unwired Planet*, which treats non-discrimination as effectively subsumed by the FR prongs.

The German Federal Court of Justice's 2020 decision in *Sisvel v. Haier II* (KZR 36/17) endorsed something close to a soft-ND view.

The English Supreme Court in *Unwired Planet* endorsed the no-ND view explicitly.

Chinese courts have not yet produced an authoritative articulation.

The UPC has not yet addressed the question.

### B. Single-Forum Global Rate-Setting

The English courts' willingness to set global rates — and to condition the avoidance of a UK injunction on the implementer's acceptance of those rates — is the single most contested feature of contemporary FRAND practice.

Critics, including the Chinese Ministry of Commerce in a 2021 public statement, have argued that this is an extra-territorial assertion of jurisdiction that violates principles of comity.

We take no position on the legitimacy of the English approach as a matter of public international law.

We do argue, in Part IX, that the global rate-setting practice creates instability that is unlikely to be cured by anti-suit and anti-anti-suit injunctions alone.

### C. Court vs. Arbitration

The relationship between judicially set rates and arbitrally set rates is undertheorized.

American practice has effectively delegated rate-setting to arbitration.

European practice has not.

Asian practice is mixed.

The result is that the same SEP holder can face, simultaneously, an English court rate determination, a Chinese court rate determination, and a private arbitration in Singapore or Hong Kong, with no clear hierarchy of precedence.

## IX. The Comity-Plus-Competence Framework

## IX. The Comity-Plus-Competence Framework

Section IX develops the authors' proposed framework over approximately 22 pages, with reference to the literature on transnational commercial law, the *Hague Choice of Court Convention*, and the doctrine of forum non conveniens.

The core of the framework is two propositions.

First, that the legitimacy of a forum's FRAND determination should depend in part on whether that forum has the institutional competence (technical expertise, procedural tools, neutrality vis-a-vis the parties) to produce a reliable result.

Second, that comity considerations — the recognition that other forums may have stronger competence or stronger ties to the dispute — should constrain a forum's willingness to make global determinations even when its formal jurisdiction permits.

## X. Conclusion

The FRAND adjudication landscape in 2026 is the product of fifteen years of doctrinal evolution that has been, in the main, ad hoc, jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction, and reactive.

The institutional incentives now point in different directions in different forums.

The result is not chaos — sophisticated parties have learned to navigate the multi-forum landscape — but it is also not stability.

We suspect that the next decade will bring further consolidation around the European and Chinese forums.

We suspect that the next decade will bring further consolidation around the European and Chinese forums.

Continued American withdrawal from judicial rate-setting.

An ongoing tension between the UPC and the English courts as the principal European venues.

The role of arbitration will grow.

The role of standards-body soft law may, finally, grow with it.

---

*Caroline E. Whitford is the Albrecht Professor of Intellectual Property Law at Berkeley Law and a faculty co-director of the Berkeley Center for Law and Technology.*

*Anjali R. Krishnaswamy is a Climenko Fellow and Lecturer on Law at Harvard Law School.*

# "I Try Not to Be the Most Expensive Person in the Room": A Q&A with Dr. Yuki Tanaka

*From* The Licensing Executive *(the quarterly magazine of the Licensing Executives Society International).*

*Volume 58, Issue 1, Spring 2024.*

*Q&A conducted by Aarti Subramaniam, LES-USA & Canada Trustee, in Tokyo on January 26, 2024.*

*Edited for length.*

---

## On a bicoastal practice

**LES: Your practice is more or less evenly divided between the United States and Japan. How did you end up with that footprint?**

Tanaka: Partly by design, mostly by accident.

I grew up in Yokohama.

I did my LL.B. at the University of Tokyo.

I assumed I was going to do corporate work in Marunouchi for the rest of my career.

I did my LL.B. at the University of Tokyo.

I assumed I was going to do corporate work in Marunouchi for the rest of my career.

Then I went to Stanford on an LL.M. and I sort of fell into the patent world and never really climbed out.

I went back to Tokyo after the doctorate to work at a U.S. firm, and I ended up doing a great deal of licensing for Japanese electronics manufacturers who were selling into the American market.

The licenses generated the disputes.

So eventually I was on a plane to San Francisco every six weeks.

That has continued for fifteen years.

## On Japan vs. the U.S.

**LES: You hold panel appointments at the JCAA, the ICDR, and SIAC. Have you observed real differences in how parties from the two sides of the Pacific approach IP arbitration?**

Tanaka: Yes — but the gap is closing.

Japanese parties were, historically, very hesitant to put a technical expert on the stand.

There was a cultural preference for written submissions and for tribunal-appointed neutrals over party-appointed ones.

American parties, of course, want full Daubert-style cross.

What I see now is a real convergence.

The Japanese in-house community has become much more comfortable with party-appointed experts.

And American counsel have learned, sometimes the hard way, that a four-day cross-examination of a Japanese inventor through a court interpreter is rarely the tactic that wins them the case.

## On caseload

**LES: How many matters do you carry at a typical moment?**

Tanaka: It varies.

## On caseload

**LES: How many matters do you carry at a typical moment?**

Tanaka: It varies.

But on a typical day I have somewhere around eight active matters where I am the sole or co-arbitrator, plus a couple of expert determinations.

Eight is about the upper bound for me if I want to remain the kind of arbitrator who actually reads the exhibits.

I have colleagues who carry twenty.

I don't know how they do it, and I don't think the parties get my best work if I try.

## On current matters

**LES: Can you discuss any current matters?**

Tanaka: Not specifically — confidentiality forbids it.

I can say in general that the matter that has taught me the most in the past year was a FRAND rate-setting in the 4G/5G area.

Three weeks of hearings.

Eleven testifying experts.

Three weeks of hearings.

Eleven testifying experts.

Two working languages.

By the end I knew more about chipset bill-of-materials accounting than I had ever expected to.

There was also a JCAA cross-license matter in the semiconductor wafer fabrication space last year that turned on a quite interesting question about whether system-on-chip products fell within the agreed definition of "licensed products."

I issued a partial award on that issue and I think it's a question we'll see again.

## On fees

**LES: Are your fees a barrier for smaller parties?**

Tanaka: I try not to be the most expensive person in the room.

My standard hourly is U.S.$1,250, which I think places me in the middle of the market for arbitrators with my profile.

My standard hourly is U.S.$1,250, which I think places me in the middle of the market for arbitrators with my profile.

For JCAA-administered matters the institution sets a fee schedule that parties find predictable.

Which I think is one of JCAA's underrated virtues.

I will sometimes cap my fees on smaller matters — under, say, ¥500 million in dispute — because I think access to qualified neutrals is a matter of basic legitimacy for the system.

The system stops working if only the very large parties can afford it.

## On teaching

**LES: You teach at Keio Law School. Given your docket, why?**

Tanaka: Because the patent litigators of twenty years from now will be the people writing the awards then.

Tanaka: Because the patent litigators of twenty years from now will be the people writing the awards then.

And at the moment there is essentially no Japanese law school course that takes semiconductor licensing seriously as a body of substantive law.

Also, frankly, the students keep me honest.

A bright 2L will ask you why an SEP holder is entitled to any royalty at all, and you have to be able to give a real answer.

If you can't, that is a useful diagnostic.

## On career advice

**LES: What advice would you give a young IP lawyer who wants to do international arbitration?**

Tanaka: Three things.

### First: learn the technology

Learn the technology.

Not at the level of a Ph.D. — you can't, and you don't need to — but well enough that you can read a claim chart without flinching.

Not at the level of a Ph.D. — you can't, and you don't need to — but well enough that you can read a claim chart without flinching.

And well enough that when an expert is being evasive you can tell.

The lawyers in this practice who are the most effective are the ones whom the engineers respect.

### Second: learn a language

Learn one foreign language well.

It doesn't have to be Japanese — Mandarin, Korean, German all work — but pick one and become genuinely fluent.

The market for fully bilingual IP arbitration counsel is structurally underserved.

### Third: build a reputation for fairness

Build a real reputation for fairness early.

The IP arbitration bar is small.

People remember.

### Third: build a reputation for fairness

Build a real reputation for fairness early.

The IP arbitration bar is small.

People remember.

The associate who two-faced a tribunal at year three will not be appointed as an arbitrator at year fifteen.

## On writing

**LES: You write extensively. What's on the desk now?**

Tanaka: I am working on a second edition of the patent disputes book.

The first edition came out in 2019 and it is well out of date, particularly on the Chinese and UPC developments.

I also have a slow-moving article on the question of whether a tribunal can or should set a global rate for a portfolio of standard-essential patents when the parties have agreed only to arbitrate a single national dispute.

The English approach is one answer.

The English approach is one answer.

The European institutional approach is another.

I am not sure either is fully satisfying.

Eventually I will figure out what I think and write it down.

## On the future

**LES: Where do you see the field in ten years?**

Tanaka: I think there will be fewer pure-play patent litigations and substantially more arbitrations.

I think the institutional market is going to consolidate around three or four serious venues — Singapore, Geneva, London, and either Tokyo or Hong Kong, depending on geopolitics.

I think the AI cases that are starting to arrive now are going to absorb more of the practice's bandwidth than anyone is currently planning for.

I think the AI cases that are starting to arrive now are going to absorb more of the practice's bandwidth than anyone is currently planning for.

And I think there is going to be a generational turnover in the neutrals pool that the institutions are not yet adequately preparing for.

The senior people are getting older.

There are not enough mid-career arbitrators with the right combination of technical fluency and procedural experience.

## On travel

**LES: Less travel?**

Tanaka: Less travel, ideally.

More writing.

The docket — the docket finds you anyway.

# "The Pacific is Narrower Than It Used to Be": A Conversation with Dr. Yuki Tanaka

*Published in the* Global Arbitration Review *Spotlight Series, 6 February 2024. Interview conducted by Priya Raman in Tokyo, 19 January 2024. Lightly edited for length and clarity.*

---

**GAR: You've built a practice that is almost evenly split between the U.S. and Japan. How did that happen?**

---

**GAR: You've built a practice that is almost evenly split between the U.S. and Japan. How did that happen?**

Tanaka: A little bit by design and a lot by accident. I grew up in Yokohama, did my LL.B. at Todai, and I always thought I would do corporate work in Tokyo. Then I went to Stanford for the LL.M. and I just — I never quite left the patent world. After the PhD I went back to Tokyo for a U.S. firm and ended up doing IP licensing for Japanese electronics companies selling into the U.S. market. The disputes followed the licenses. So now I am on a plane to SFO basically every six weeks.

**GAR: You sit on the JCAA, ICDR, and SIAC panels. Do you see real differences in how parties from the two sides of the Pacific approach IP arbitration?**

Tanaka: Yes, but the gap is narrowing. Japanese parties used to be very reluctant to put technical experts on the stand — there was a cultural preference for written submissions and tribunal-appointed neutrals. U.S. parties of course want full Daubert-style cross. What I see now is convergence: Japanese in-house teams are much more comfortable with party-appointed experts, and U.S. counsel have learned that a four-day cross-examination of a Japanese inventor through interpretation is not always their best tactic.

**GAR: Roughly how many matters are you handling at any one time?**

**GAR: Roughly how many matters are you handling at any one time?**

Tanaka: It varies. On a typical day I have around eight active matters where I'm sole or co-arbitrator, plus a couple of expert determinations. Eight is about my ceiling if I want to be the kind of arbitrator who actually reads the exhibits.

**GAR: Can you talk about any of them?**

**GAR: Can you talk about any of them?**

Tanaka: Not specifically. I can say the recent matter that has taught me the most was a FRAND rate-setting in the 4G/5G space — three weeks of hearings, eleven experts, two languages. By the end I knew more about chipset bill-of-materials accounting than I ever wanted to. There was also a JCAA cross-license dispute in the semiconductor wafer space last year that involved a very interesting question about whether system-on-chip products fell within the licensed-products definition. I issued a partial award on that.

**GAR: Are your fees a barrier for smaller parties?**

**GAR: Are your fees a barrier for smaller parties?**

Tanaka: I try not to be the most expensive person in the room. My standard hourly rate is U.S.$1,250, which I think is in the middle of the market for arbitrators with my profile. For JCAA-administered cases the institution sets a schedule that the parties find predictable. I will sometimes cap fees on smaller matters — under, say, ¥500 million in dispute — because I think access to high-quality neutrals matters.

**GAR: You teach at Keio. Why bother, given your docket?**

**GAR: You teach at Keio. Why bother, given your docket?**

Tanaka: Because patent litigators in twenty years will be the ones writing these awards, and right now there is essentially no Japanese law school course that takes semiconductor licensing seriously. Also, the students keep me honest. A bright 2L will ask you why a SEP holder gets a royalty at all, and you have to be able to answer.

**GAR: What's next?**

Tanaka: Less travel, ideally. More writing. I'm working on a second edition of the patent disputes book. And the docket — the docket always finds you.

---

*Dr. Tanaka is an independent arbitrator based in Palo Alto and Tokyo.*

# Tanaka Tapped to Chair Tribunal in $900M U.S.–Japan Chip Patent Spat

*Managing IP — 22 November 2023*
*By Hannah Ueno, Tokyo Correspondent*

Veteran arbitrator Dr. Yuki Tanaka has been confirmed as president of a three-member ICDR tribunal in what sources describe as one of the largest semiconductor patent disputes of the year, pitting a U.S.-listed memory company against a Japanese equipment manufacturer over a long-running cross-license. The amount in controversy is understood to exceed U.S.$900 million.

Tanaka, who splits her practice between Palo Alto and Tokyo, was nominated by the two party-appointed arbitrators after the parties failed to agree on a chair within the contractual window. People familiar with the matter say her dual U.S.–Japan qualification and her facility with technical evidence in both languages were determinative. The arbitration is seated in Singapore and will apply New York law.

"Yuki is one of the very few arbitrators who can run a wafer-fab patent case in two languages without losing a beat," said one senior counsel at a Magic Circle firm in Tokyo, who has appeared before her twice. "She runs a tight procedural ship and she actually reads the prior art."

Tanaka, 48, took her doctorate at Stanford Law School in 2010, where her dissertation work on FRAND obligations in cross-border licensing has since been cited by Japanese and Korean courts. Before turning full-time to arbitration in 2015, she practiced in the Tokyo office of a U.S. firm, where she advised on the licensing programs of several major Japanese electronics conglomerates. She holds bar admissions in California and as a Japanese bengoshi, and is registered as a gaikokuho-jimu-bengoshi in Tokyo.

She sits on the panels of the JCAA, AAA-ICDR, SIAC, and most recently — as of last spring — the Hong Kong International Arbitration Centre, where she was added to the IP-specialist roster. She is also an adjunct professor at Keio University, where she co-teaches a popular seminar on semiconductor licensing.

Industry observers note that the new appointment is consistent with a recent trend of high-value Asia-seated patent arbitrations being chaired by arbitrators with deep semiconductor sector knowledge. Tanaka's recent docket has reportedly included a JCAA-administered cross-licensing dispute involving a Japanese IDM and a U.S. fabless designer (publicly believed to be in the ¥18 billion range), and a FRAND rate-setting dispute over a 4G/5G SEP portfolio.

In a brief comment to *Managing IP*, Tanaka declined to discuss the new appointment but observed: "There is more cross-border IP arbitration in this corridor than there was five years ago, and the cases are more technically complex. That is good for the parties — courts are not always set up for this."

A first procedural conference in the new ICDR matter is scheduled for early December. A merits hearing is not expected before late 2024.

# Partial Final Award (Excerpt)

**JCAA Arbitration Case No. 22-074**

**In the Matter of an Arbitration Between:**

**Sakura Microelectronics Corporation** (Claimant)
— and —
**Pacific Logic Devices, Inc.** (Respondent)

Seat of Arbitration: Tokyo, Japan
Governing Law: California law (substantive); JCAA Commercial Arbitration Rules 2021 (procedural)
Tribunal: **Dr. Yuki Tanaka, Sole Arbitrator**

Date of Partial Final Award: **14 March 2023**

---

## I. Introduction and Procedural History

Date of Partial Final Award: **14 March 2023**

---

## I. Introduction and Procedural History

1. This Partial Final Award is rendered in an arbitration commenced by Sakura Microelectronics Corporation ("Sakura" or "Claimant"), a kabushiki kaisha organized under the laws of Japan with its principal place of business in Yokohama, against Pacific Logic Devices, Inc. ("PLD" or "Respondent"), a Delaware corporation with its principal place of business in San Jose, California.

2. The dispute arises out of the Patent Cross-License and Foundry Supply Agreement dated 1 April 2018 (the "PCLA"), pursuant to which Sakura granted PLD a non-exclusive license to a portfolio of 287 patents in the field of low-power CMOS logic and embedded SRAM (the "Licensed Patents"), in exchange for running royalties calculated on Net Sales of Licensed Products and a most-favored-licensee ("MFL") undertaking set forth at Article 7.3.

3. The Request for Arbitration was filed on 11 May 2022. Following the parties' agreement, the undersigned was confirmed as Sole Arbitrator on 8 July 2022. A procedural hearing was held by videoconference on 22 July 2022, at which Procedural Order No. 1 was issued. Evidentiary hearings were conducted in Tokyo from 9 to 13 January 2023, with simultaneous interpretation into Japanese and English.

## II. The Dispute in Summary

## II. The Dispute in Summary

4. Claimant contends that Respondent has materially underreported Net Sales for fiscal years 2019 through 2021 by improperly excluding revenue from "system-on-chip" products in which the Licensed Patents are embodied, on the theory that such SoCs fall outside the definition of "Licensed Products" in Section 1.14 of the PCLA. Claimant seeks (i) a declaration that the SoC products are Licensed Products; (ii) payment of unpaid royalties in the amount of U.S.$71,400,000, plus pre-award interest; and (iii) an order directing a forensic audit pursuant to Article 9.

5. Respondent denies the foregoing and counterclaims that Claimant breached the MFL clause by extending a 1.25% royalty rate to a third-party licensee (a Korean memory manufacturer) in December 2020, while continuing to invoice Respondent at the contractual 2.10% rate.

## III. Findings on Contract Interpretation

## III. Findings on Contract Interpretation

6. The Tribunal has carefully considered the textual, contextual, and negotiating-history evidence under California Civil Code §§ 1636–1647. The definition of "Licensed Products" at Section 1.14 is, on its face, broad: it captures "any integrated circuit, the manufacture, use, sale, or import of which would, but for the licenses granted hereunder, infringe one or more Valid Claims of the Licensed Patents." The Tribunal does not accept Respondent's argument that the parties' course of dealing in 2016–2017, under a predecessor agreement, narrows that definition. As the California Supreme Court has observed, course of dealing cannot vary unambiguous express terms. *See Pacific Gas & Elec. Co. v. G.W. Thomas Drayage & Rigging Co.*, 69 Cal. 2d 33 (1968).

7. Accordingly, the Tribunal finds that the SoC products identified in Claimant's Exhibit C-114 are Licensed Products within the meaning of Section 1.14.

## IV. Findings on the MFL Counterclaim

8. The MFL clause is engaged. However, the Tribunal finds that the rate differential is partially justified by the differing scope of the third-party grant (which excludes foundry rights). The royalty owed by Respondent shall be adjusted prospectively to 1.65%, effective from the date of the third-party license.

# Stanford Law School Announces New Center for IP Innovation and Policy, Names Inaugural Faculty Directors

## Lead

Stanford, California — Stanford Law School today announced the launch of the Stanford Center for IP Innovation and Policy (CIPIP).

The Center is a new research center that will serve as the school's principal hub for interdisciplinary scholarship and policy engagement on intellectual property law in an era of rapid technological change.

The Center will be co-directed by Professor Mark Lemley, the William H. Neukom Professor of Law, and Professor Lisa Larrimore Ouellette, the Deane F. Johnson Professor of Law.

An initial gift of $42 million from the Hoffman-Yee Family Foundation funds the launch.

---

## Mission

The Center for IP Innovation and Policy will conduct empirical and doctrinal research on intellectual property law.

It will host visiting scholars and practitioners from around the world.

And engage directly with policymakers in the United States and internationally.

Its initial research agenda will focus on five priority areas.

### Priority area 1: AI and IP

The interaction between artificial intelligence and the existing IP framework.

Including questions of training-data licensing, output attribution, model patenting, and the application of fair use doctrine to model training.

### Priority area 2: Empirical patent enforcement

### Priority area 2: Empirical patent enforcement

The empirical study of patent enforcement and licensing.

Building on the work of the Stanford NPE Litigation Database.

### Priority area 3: Standards and FRAND

The economic and legal evaluation of standard-setting and the FRAND regime.

With particular attention to telecommunications and automotive standards.

### Priority area 4: Pharma and biotech

Pharmaceutical and biotechnology patent policy.

Including the operation of the Hatch-Waxman framework and the Biologics Price Competition and Innovation Act.

And the interaction between patent term and FDA exclusivities.

### Priority area 5: Trade secrets

Trade secret law in the era of remote work and global supply chains.

## Opening scene

**SINGAPORE / TOKYO / SEOUL / SHENZHEN —** On the 39th floor of a glass tower in Singapore's Maxwell Chambers Suites, in a windowless conference room reserved months in advance, three arbitrators in dark suits spent the second week of February quietly setting a global royalty rate for one of the most-licensed patent portfolios in the wireless industry.

The hearing ran for nine working days.

The hearing ran for nine working days.

## The new geography

This is the new geography of intellectual property dispute resolution.

Opaque.

Private.

Expensive.

## The new geography

This is the new geography of intellectual property dispute resolution.

Opaque.

Private.

Expensive.

And increasingly Asian.

Across the Asia-Pacific region — in Singapore, Hong Kong, Tokyo, Seoul, Shenzhen, and Sydney — a network of arbitral institutions, specialist counsel, and a few dozen trusted arbitrators has, over the past fifteen years, displaced public courts as the principal venue for resolving the most consequential disputes in the global technology economy.

The rise of these tribunals has been gradual and largely unremarked.

There is no press conference when a major SEP dispute is settled by arbitration.

There is no docket to read.

The awards, when they issue, are not published.

Even the existence of a dispute is often confidential.

There is no docket to read.

The awards, when they issue, are not published.

Even the existence of a dispute is often confidential.

The result is that the largest single-asset commercial determinations made anywhere in the world are now made, routinely, in proceedings that the public never sees and rarely hears about.

This feature is an attempt to describe the geography of those proceedings.

Who is involved.

Where they happen.

Why parties choose them.

And what the rise of private dispute resolution means for the broader trajectory of innovation policy in the region.

## I. The institutions

The four most-used institutional arbitration providers for technology licensing disputes in the Asia-Pacific region, by case volume, are:

The Singapore International Arbitration Centre (SIAC).

The Singapore International Arbitration Centre (SIAC).

The Hong Kong International Arbitration Centre (HKIAC).

The Japan Commercial Arbitration Association (JCAA).

And — increasingly — the China International Economic and Trade Arbitration Commission (CIETAC).

### SIAC

SIAC, founded in 1991 and modernized aggressively under the leadership of its long-time CEO Lim Seok Hui, administered 1,142 cases in 2023.

Of these, approximately 18 percent (an estimate based on SIAC's own caseload report) involved intellectual property or technology-licensing subject matter.

A share that has grown steadily from approximately 9 percent in 2015.

The institution does not break out SEP-related cases separately.

### HKIAC

HKIAC, somewhat older and historically more conservative, administered 503 cases in 2023.

Hong Kong's particular advantage is the New Arrangement on Reciprocal Recognition and Enforcement of Arbitral Awards with the People's Republic of China.

Which gives HKIAC awards a uniquely smooth path into enforcement in the Chinese mainland.

An advantage that matters greatly when one of the parties is Chinese and assets are located there.

### JCAA

An advantage that matters greatly when one of the parties is Chinese and assets are located there.

### JCAA

JCAA, the Japanese institution, administered 76 cases in 2023.

Which is small by international comparison but represents a meaningful share of the cross-border patent licensing disputes involving Japanese parties.

JCAA has, under its recently appointed Secretary-General Takashi Hattori, positioned itself as a specialist venue for IP disputes.

With a curated roster of arbitrators including former judges of the IP High Court and a handful of well-known international neutrals.

### CIETAC

With a curated roster of arbitrators including former judges of the IP High Court and a handful of well-known international neutrals.

### CIETAC

CIETAC, the largest of the four by total caseload (5,237 cases in 2023, of which only a small fraction were IP-related), has historically been used principally for Chinese-counterparty commercial disputes.

Its growth in technology cases has been recent and is concentrated in disputes where one or both parties are Chinese.

The number of people who can credibly chair a complex SEP arbitration involving telecommunications standards is small.

Among the most-appointed arbitrators in Asia-Pacific IP disputes, based on publicly available appointment records and interviews with eleven sitting practitioners conducted for this feature, are:

## III. The substance

Across roughly thirty interviews conducted for this feature with arbitrators, counsel, in-house lawyers at major Asian and European technology companies, and academic observers, several recurring substantive themes emerged.

### Comparable licenses

Comparable licenses are the dominant valuation methodology.

### Comparable licenses

Comparable licenses are the dominant valuation methodology.

Notwithstanding the academic literature's interest in bottom-up valuation approaches based on patent counts and incremental value, practitioners report that the substantial majority of FRAND arbitral determinations in the region rely principally on comparable license benchmarking.

One London-based counsel who has appeared in seven such arbitrations described the methodology as "find the most truly comparable license, argue about why it is or is not comparable, and produce a rate."

### A small expert pool

The pool of competent technical experts is small.

### Awards take longer than advertised

Awards take longer than the institutional timelines suggest.

SIAC's expedited procedure target of six months from constitution to award is, in practice, met in only a minority of complex SEP cases.

Most cases run twelve to twenty-four months.

Several practitioners attributed this to the complexity of the underlying material.

### Confidentiality is uneven

Although parties and arbitrators uniformly support confidentiality, the practical reality is that information about cases circulates through the small specialist bar.

Counsel who appear in one case for an SEP holder typically know, in general terms, the rates being argued in another case for the same SEP holder.

Whether this is a feature (efficient information aggregation) or a problem (informal collusion on rates) is debated.

## IV. The public-court alternative

## IV. The public-court alternative

The principal public-court alternatives to private arbitration in the region are:

The Tokyo IP High Court.

The Beijing IP Court and the Supreme People's Court of China.

The Korean IP High Court.

And the High Court of Singapore.

Of these, the Chinese courts have most aggressively asserted global rate-setting jurisdiction.

With mixed reception elsewhere.

Several practitioners interviewed for this feature predicted that the Chinese courts' jurisdictional assertions would push more disputes into arbitration.

On the theory that sophisticated parties prefer the perceived neutrality and confidentiality of arbitration to the risks of a Chinese court rate-setting on a global portfolio.

This prediction has, so far, been at least partially borne out by the caseload trends.

## V. The future

The region's IP arbitration practice is at an inflection point.

Three trends, each independent, may shape its evolution over the next decade.

### Trend 1: AI

First, the rise of AI-related licensing disputes.

Between model developers and training-data rightsholders.

Between model owners and downstream API customers.

Between owners of patented neural-network architectures and implementers.

Is producing a new generation of cases whose technical and legal questions are substantially different from the SEP disputes of the past fifteen years.

### Trend 2: Patent pools

### Trend 2: Patent pools

Second, the growth of patent pools as a licensing mechanism (Avanci, Sisvel, Via Licensing) is changing the bilateral negotiation dynamics that have historically driven into arbitration.

Pool-administered licenses produce a different shape of dispute when they produce one at all.

### Trend 3: Geopolitical fragmentation

Third, the broader geopolitical fragmentation of the technology economy is generating disputes whose jurisdictional and substantive complexity exceed anything the existing institutional framework was designed for.

Whether the institutions can adapt is, in the view of several senior practitioners interviewed for this feature, an open question.

## VI. Coda: A view from the inside

## VI. Coda: A view from the inside

I spoke, finally, with a senior in-house IP counsel at a major Japanese consumer electronics company, who agreed to speak on the condition that the company not be named.

He said something that has stayed with me.

"The thing the public doesn't understand," he said, "is that almost none of this is adversarial in the way litigation is adversarial."

"The companies are in continuous, multi-decade, multi-product relationships."

"We are in arbitration with the same companies we are jointly developing chipsets with."

"The arbitration is one move in a relationship that has thirty other moves going on at the same time."

"The arbitrator who understands that — the one who sees the relationship and not just the case — that is the one we want."

He paused.

"The arbitrator who understands that — the one who sees the relationship and not just the case — that is the one we want."

He paused.

"There are only a few of them. We are not going to tell you who."

*Published April 2024 by the World Intellectual Property Organization, Geneva. Document reference: WIPO/AMC/AR/2023.*

## Foreword by the Director of the Arbitration and Mediation Center

---

## Foreword by the Director of the Arbitration and Mediation Center

This is the twenty-eighth annual caseload report of the WIPO Arbitration and Mediation Center. As in previous years, it reports on the Center's activity across its three principal lines of work: (1) administration of arbitration, mediation, expert determination, and other alternative dispute resolution procedures under the WIPO Rules; (2) administration of domain-name dispute resolution proceedings under the Uniform Domain Name Dispute Resolution Policy (UDRP) and the ccTLD-specific policies for which the Center is the designated provider; and (3) policy work, capacity-building, and the maintenance of the WIPO neutrals roster.

2023 was a year of consolidation rather than dramatic change. The Center's commercial IP caseload grew modestly (8.2 percent year-on-year). Its domain-name caseload, which has now exceeded 6,000 cases per year for eight consecutive years, was essentially flat. The most significant developments were procedural rather than statistical: the entry into force of the revised WIPO Mediation, Arbitration, Expedited Arbitration and Expert Determination Rules in July 2023, and the launch of a new pilot program for the resolution of disputes involving artificial intelligence and generative-content patents in October 2023.

This report is intended primarily for users of the Center's services — parties, counsel, neutrals, and the broader IP and dispute-resolution community. It is not a research publication and does not attempt to draw strong conclusions from the data. Where comparisons across years are made, methodology is described in the appendix.

## 6. Procedural Rule Revisions

The revised WIPO Mediation, Arbitration, Expedited Arbitration and Expert Determination Rules entered into force on July 1, 2023. The principal changes are summarized below; a comprehensive comparative table is available in Annex B.

### 6.1 Tribunal-led case management

The revised Rules formalize the tribunal's authority to conduct an early case management conference (within 30 days of constitution) and to issue a procedural order setting time limits, document production parameters, and witness arrangements. This codifies practice that was already widely followed but had no explicit textual basis.

### 6.2 Remote hearings

### 6.2 Remote hearings

The revised Rules contain an express provision (Article 41) on remote and hybrid hearings, drawing on practice developed during the COVID-19 period. The default rule is that the tribunal may direct that any or all of a hearing be conducted by videoconference, with reasonable notice and after consultation with the parties.

### 6.3 Third-party funding disclosure

The revised Rules introduce, for the first time at WIPO, a mandatory disclosure obligation (Article 11) for third-party funding arrangements. Parties must disclose the existence and identity of any third-party funder within 21 days of constitution of the tribunal or, if funding is obtained later, within 21 days of obtaining it.

### 6.4 Expedited procedure threshold

### 6.4 Expedited procedure threshold

The threshold for the default applicability of the Expedited Arbitration Rules has been raised from US$10 million to US$15 million in amount in dispute, reflecting general inflation and feedback from users.

## 7. AI-Related Disputes Pilot

In October 2023, the Center launched a two-year pilot program for the resolution of disputes involving artificial intelligence and generative-content patents and licensing. The pilot offers (1) a curated sub-roster of AI-specialized neutrals (initially 38 individuals); (2) model dispute resolution clauses for AI training-data licensing and model-output licensing agreements; and (3) a fast-track procedure with a six-month default timeline from commencement to award.

As of December 31, 2023, three cases had been filed under the pilot. The Center will report on the pilot's first-year results in the 2024 annual report.

## 8. Capacity-Building and Outreach

The Center delivered 31 training programs in 2023, reaching approximately 2,800 participants. Notable programs included the WIPO-WTO Colloquium for IP Teachers (Geneva, June 2023), the Asia-Pacific IP Arbitration Conference (Singapore, September 2023, co-organized with SIAC), and a new program on IP and the metaverse (virtual, November 2023).

The Center published two new model contract clauses (for AI training-data licensing and for generative-content licensing), one new procedural guide (on the conduct of expert determination in patent disputes), and three new podcast episodes in its *Mediation Stories* series.

## 9. Financial Data

The Center is self-funded through procedural fees and operates within the broader WIPO budget. Total revenue from procedural fees in 2023 was 14.8 million Swiss francs, of which approximately 9.2 million came from domain-name proceedings and 5.6 million from commercial cases. The Center operated with a surplus of approximately 1.3 million Swiss francs, which is returned to WIPO general revenue.

## 10. Looking Ahead

## 10. Looking Ahead

The Center expects 2024 to be a year of continued growth in commercial caseload, particularly in software/IT licensing and pharmaceutical patent disputes. Domain-name caseload is expected to remain stable, with continued growth in .ai disputes. The Center will report on the AI-related disputes pilot's first-year results, will publish a new set of recommended clauses for trade-secret disputes, and will continue to expand its neutrals roster with particular attention to underrepresented regions.

For party-counting purposes, each natural or legal person identified as a claimant, respondent, or joined party is counted once per case.

### A.3 Subject-matter classification

The 2023 Rules adjust several time limits relative to the 2020 Rules.

The 2023 Rules require the tribunal to address costs in the final award.

## Announcement

The University of Tokyo Department of Physics is pleased to announce that Professor Yuki Tanaka has been awarded the 2025 Nishina Memorial Prize.

Tanaka is a member of the Department of Astronomy and the Kavli Institute for the Physics and Mathematics of the Universe (Kavli IPMU).

The prize is awarded for his contributions to the study of galaxy formation in the early universe.

The prize is awarded for his contributions to the study of galaxy formation in the early universe.

In particular, his use of the James Webb Space Telescope to characterize the rest-frame ultraviolet spectra of galaxies at redshifts greater than ten.

## About the prize

The Nishina Memorial Prize was established in 1955.

It honors Yoshio Nishina (1890–1951).

It is one of the most prestigious awards in Japanese physics.

Past recipients include twelve scientists who subsequently received the Nobel Prize in Physics.

## Biographical sketch

Professor Tanaka, 54, was born in Sendai.

He received his B.Sc. (1993) and Ph.D. (1998) from the University of Tokyo.

## Biographical sketch

Professor Tanaka, 54, was born in Sendai.

He received his B.Sc. (1993) and Ph.D. (1998) from the University of Tokyo.

His doctoral dissertation, supervised by the late Professor Sadanori Okamura, characterized the morphology of galaxies in the Hubble Deep Field North using ground-based imaging and the then-new HST data.

He held postdoctoral positions at the Max Planck Institute for Astrophysics in Garching (1998–2001).

And the Space Telescope Science Institute in Baltimore (2001–2004).

Before joining the Subaru Telescope as a staff astronomer in Hawai'i (2004–2009).

He returned to Japan in 2009 as an associate professor at the National Astronomical Observatory of Japan (NAOJ).

He led the Hyper Suprime-Cam strategic survey program from 2014 to 2019.

He led the Hyper Suprime-Cam strategic survey program from 2014 to 2019.

He was appointed full professor at the University of Tokyo in 2018.

He joined Kavli IPMU as a principal investigator in 2020.

## Productivity

He has authored or co-authored more than 340 refereed publications.

His h-index is 72 (as of January 2025).

He has supervised eleven Ph.D. students to completion.

## Cited work

The Nishina Memorial Foundation cited three contributions in particular.

### Contribution 1: The HSC quasar survey

The Subaru Hyper Suprime-Cam survey of z > 6 quasars.

Tanaka led the team that, between 2014 and 2021, discovered approximately 230 quasars at redshifts greater than six in a 1,400 square-degree imaging survey.

Including the second-most-distant known quasar, HSC J2331+0034, at z = 7.31.

This work has constrained the bright end of the high-redshift quasar luminosity function.

And, by extension, the timing of the epoch of reionization.

### Contribution 2: The JADES Origins Field

The JADES Origins Field deep spectroscopic survey.

Tanaka is a co-principal investigator (with Daniel Eisenstein of the Center for Astrophysics) on the JWST Advanced Deep Extragalactic Survey.

Which between 2022 and 2024 obtained NIRSpec spectra of more than 5,000 galaxies at z > 5.

He led the team that confirmed the spectroscopic redshift of JADES-GS-z13-1 at z = 13.2 in October 2023.

Which at the time was the most distant spectroscopically confirmed galaxy.

### Contribution 3: Population III stars

Which at the time was the most distant spectroscopically confirmed galaxy.

### Contribution 3: Population III stars

Theoretical work on Population III star formation.

In collaboration with Naoki Yoshida (University of Tokyo) and Volker Bromm (UT Austin).

Tanaka has produced a series of papers between 2016 and 2024 modeling the formation, lifetime, and chemical signatures of the first generation of stars.

The most-cited of these, "On the Initial Mass Function of Population III Stars in Lyman-Werner-Suppressed Halos" (Tanaka, Yoshida, & Bromm 2019, ApJ 885, 142), has been cited more than 410 times.

## Public engagement

Tanaka has been an active public communicator of science, particularly in Japan.

He has appeared on NHK's *Cosmic Front* program seven times since 2015.

He has appeared on NHK's *Cosmic Front* program seven times since 2015.

He hosted the NHK Educational documentary *The First Light* (2023, 90 minutes).

He authored two trade books.

*Looking Back in Time* (Iwanami Shinsho, 2020, 248 pages, sold approximately 92,000 copies).

*The Universe in the First Billion Years* (Kodansha Bluebacks, 2024, 312 pages).

He is a frequent contributor to the magazine *Newton*.

He writes a monthly column on cosmology for the *Asahi Shimbun* Sunday science page.

## On confusion with other public figures

Tanaka has commented, in interviews and on his personal website (https://tanaka-cosmology.phys.s.u-tokyo.ac.jp), that he is occasionally confused with several other public figures who share his name.

Including a U.S.-based arbitrator who practices in intellectual property disputes.

A wildlife photographer based in Hokkaido.

And most recently a chef whose Berkeley restaurant has received favorable press.

He notes drily on his FAQ page: "I am none of these people."

"The arbitrator and I were briefly mistakenly invoiced for each other's hotel rooms at a Tokyo conference in 2021."

"We resolved this amicably over coffee."

## A further clarification

He has further clarified, in response to a 2023 email from a journalism student who was researching the wrong Tanaka entirely.

"I have no expertise in standard-essential patents, FRAND licensing, or any other matter of intellectual property law."

"I have not eaten at the Berkeley restaurant."

"I have not eaten at the Berkeley restaurant."

"I do photograph the sky, but not professionally and not very well."

## Award details

The Nishina Memorial Prize carries a cash award of ¥1,000,000 and a commemorative medal.

The award ceremony will be held at the Nishina Memorial Foundation in Bunkyo-ku, Tokyo, on December 6, 2025.

The prize lecture, "Reaching for the First Light: A Personal Account of JWST's First Three Years," will be delivered on the same date.

It is open to the public.

Registration opens November 1, 2025.

## Comments from colleagues

### Yoshida

"Yuki has been the central figure in Japanese extragalactic astronomy for the past fifteen years," said Professor Naoki Yoshida, director of Kavli IPMU.

"His combination of observational leadership and theoretical insight is unusual."

"The JADES-GS-z13-1 result, in particular, was a genuinely transformative measurement, and he was the right person to lead the team that produced it."

"Yuki's HSC quasar survey work is what every survey wants to be," said Professor Masami Ouchi (NAOJ).

"I am embarrassed by this prize because I share it with the entire team," said Tanaka, when reached by phone for comment.

"JWST data analysis is not done alone."

"I would like to thank my co-investigators, my students, my long-suffering wife, and the engineers at STScI who actually fly the telescope."

## The headline

I have eaten at Hinoki & Salt four times since it opened in the converted bicycle-repair shop on Ashby Avenue in February. I have paid the full $385 each time, the Chronicle's policy.

I am awarding it three stars — our highest rating, the first three-star debut in the East Bay since Chez Panisse in the mid-1990s — and I am doing so without much hesitation, despite a service program that is still finding its footing and a wine list that, frankly, needs a buyer who is not also the chef.

This is the restaurant of the year.

## The chef

Tanaka, 38, is not a household name yet, but he is not exactly a newcomer either.

This is the restaurant of the year.

## The chef

Tanaka, 38, is not a household name yet, but he is not exactly a newcomer either.

He was born in Fukuoka and raised partly in Osaka. He came to California at 22 to study at the Culinary Institute of America's Greystone campus, then worked the line at Manresa under David Kinch for three years.

He moved to State Bird Provisions as a sous chef in 2017 and stayed through the pandemic — the period during which, he told me, he "learned how to be Californian."

He spent 2022 staging in Kyoto at Kikunoi and a small unnamed kappo in Pontocho whose chef, he says, taught him "the difference between technique and intention."

Hinoki & Salt is his first restaurant.

## The room

Hinoki & Salt is his first restaurant.

## The room

It seats eight at the counter, four at a single small table, and turns twice a night, Tuesday through Saturday.

The reservation system opens at 9 a.m. on the first of each month.

The waitlist is reportedly in the four digits.

The room is austere in a way that initially reads as severe and, over the course of an evening, begins to feel generous.

The counter is a single 14-foot slab of hinoki cypress, sourced — Tanaka volunteers, almost shyly — from a forester in Kiso whom he visited in 2023.

The walls are unfinished lime plaster the color of wet sand.

There is one piece of art: a hanging scroll by the Kyoto calligrapher Sasaki Michiko, changed every two weeks.

There is one piece of art: a hanging scroll by the Kyoto calligrapher Sasaki Michiko, changed every two weeks.

The lighting is low and warm enough that I had to ask three times for the bill to be re-read to me.

The acoustics are slightly too live; conversation across the counter carries.

Service, led by general manager Aiko Yamashita (most recently of Single Thread), is technically precise but still a little stiff.

Two of my four visits featured awkward pacing issues — a 22-minute gap between courses six and seven on one Wednesday, a rush through the final three courses on a Saturday.

These are fixable.

## The food

The menu is what Tanaka calls "Berkeley kappo," and the term is not pretension.

These are fixable.

## The food

The menu is what Tanaka calls "Berkeley kappo," and the term is not pretension.

Kappo, as he explains in a brief printed note on the back of the menu, is the older and less codified cousin of kaiseki — a counter-driven format in which the chef cooks in front of you and the meal is shaped by what arrived that morning from the boat, the field, or the forager.

Tanaka's interpretation is unmistakably Californian in its sourcing — Monterey Bay spot prawns, Hog Island oysters, Chino Farm vegetables, Capay Valley citrus — and unmistakably Japanese in its restraint and its relationship to fire.

### The dashi

A bowl of dashi made from four-year-aged kombu from Rishiri and katsuobushi from a single shaver in Shizuoka.

Poured tableside over a piece of monkfish liver that had been cured in sake lees for nine days.

It tasted, improbably, like the ocean at dawn.

### The yuba

A single sheet of house-made yuba, lifted from a small copper pot at the counter and set on the plate still steaming.

Three grains of finishing salt.

One drop of barrel-aged soy.

Three ingredients. I think about it weekly.

### The wagyu

A piece of charcoal-grilled A5 Miyazaki wagyu, sliced thin and laid over a bed of hot rice from a cast-iron pot, with grated wasabi from a Half Moon Bay farm.

Not innovative. Perfectly executed.

### The pre-dessert

Not innovative. Perfectly executed.

### The pre-dessert

Green strawberries from Knoll Farms, lightly pickled in sansho-leaf vinegar.

Served in a chilled ceramic dish with a single shiso flower.

This is the dish that made me decide on the third star.

### The full menu

The eighteen-course menu is too long to recount fully, and Tanaka changes roughly a third of it weekly.

There were two dishes, across four visits, that did not work.

An October course pairing matsutake mushrooms with a too-sweet persimmon vinaigrette.

A November dish of grilled ayu whose bitterness, ordinarily the point, was here muted by an unnecessary citrus oil.

Both were misfires in service of an ambitious idea, which is the kind of misfire I will forgive in a chef this young.

## The wine

The wine list, written by Tanaka himself, is short and idiosyncratic.

Heavy on Burgundy whites and Loire reds.

Almost entirely without sake — a strange choice for a kappo restaurant.

The pairings ($245 per person) are well-considered but conservative.

I would like to see Hinoki & Salt hire a beverage director by next year.

Tanaka admits, when pressed, that this is on his list.

Optional pairings of wine ($245) or non-alcoholic beverages ($95).

Reservations require a non-refundable $200 deposit.

This is among the most expensive meals in the Bay Area.

I think it is worth the money in the same way that the very best meals at Manresa, or The French Laundry, or n/naka in Los Angeles are worth the money: not because the ingredients are fancy, but because the chef is doing something that you cannot eat anywhere else.

## A note on the chef's name

Several readers have written in, after the restaurant's soft-opening press, to ask whether this Yuki Tanaka is the same Yuki Tanaka who is a well-known arbitrator on the international IP scene.

He is not.

He is not.

The chef Tanaka, when I asked him about this at the counter on my second visit, laughed and said, "I get his email sometimes. He probably gets mine. I hope he is eating well."

He added, after a pause: "I think he is taller."

## A second note on identity

There are, by my count, at least three semi-public figures named Yuki Tanaka who have been confused for one another in the past few years.

The chef.

The arbitrator.

A wildlife photographer based in Hokkaido whose work I admire from afar.

I suppose this is what happens with a common name.

# The Cranes That Stayed: Yuki Tanaka and the Photographic Witness of Hokkaido's Vanishing Winter

*A monograph essay by Helena Brandt.*

*Originally published in* Aperture *No. 254 (Winter 2024), pp. 88–117.*

*Reproduced here with permission. Copyright 2024 Helena Brandt and the Aperture Foundation.*

---

## I. The thing that matters

Yuki Tanaka does not photograph the red-crowned crane the way other photographers photograph the red-crowned crane.

This is the first thing to understand.

It is the only thing that matters.

For more than two decades, the wetlands of eastern Hokkaido — Kushiro, Tsurui, Akan — have been one of the most heavily photographed wildlife sites on earth.

The cranes (tancho, in Japanese) are spectacular.

A meter and a half tall.

White bodies.

Black necks.

The cranes (tancho, in Japanese) are spectacular.

A meter and a half tall.

White bodies.

Black necks.

A crimson crown that in flight is almost unreasonably beautiful against the blue-shadowed snow.

They dance.

They are monogamous, theatrical, and large.

There are now perhaps 1,900 of them in Hokkaido, up from a population reduced by 1924 to fewer than two dozen birds.

Photographers from across the world arrive in January and February to stand behind tripods at the well-known feeding stations and produce images that, taken together, would fill a hangar.

Tanaka's photographs are not those images.

## II. What he does not shoot

He does not shoot at the feeding stations.

He does not shoot the dance.

Tanaka's photographs are not those images.

## II. What he does not shoot

He does not shoot at the feeding stations.

He does not shoot the dance.

He does not shoot the famous mid-air territorial display that any tour group can witness from the wooden viewing platform at Tsurui Ito Sanctuary on any clear morning between 8 and 10 a.m.

## III. What he does shoot

He shoots, instead, what happens at the edges.

A single crane standing alone in a fog so thick the bird is more rumor than presence.

The black silhouette of a parent and chick crossing a frozen river in late afternoon, the ice the same color as the sky, the birds the only thing in the frame that is not a horizontal line.

A pair asleep, necks tucked, on a snowbank where the wind has carved a row of small parallel ridges that look like the strokes of a brush.

And — most famously — the photograph that appeared on the cover of *National Geographic*'s January 2022 issue.

A single crane in flight at dusk.

Low over the pack ice off the Notsuke Peninsula.

The bird almost entirely in silhouette except for a single point of red at the crown that catches the last light.

That photograph took him eleven winters to make.

## IV. Biography

Tanaka was born in Abashiri, on the northeast coast of Hokkaido, in 1976.

His father was a commercial fisherman who worked the Sea of Okhotsk in spring and summer and repaired boats in winter.

His mother taught primary-school art.

His mother taught primary-school art.

Tanaka has said, in the few interviews he has given, that he learned to photograph by sitting on the rocks at the edge of the harbor with a Pentax K1000 his uncle had given him for his twelfth birthday, "waiting for the light to do something interesting, which in Abashiri it does roughly four hours per day, six months per year."

He studied at Tokyo University of the Arts.

Graphic design, not photography — he never had formal training in photography.

He worked for ten years as an art director at a Tokyo advertising agency, photographing only on weekends and during his annual two-week home visit to Hokkaido.

He sold his first wildlife photograph, of a Steller's sea eagle on pack ice, to a Japanese conservation magazine in 2004 for the equivalent of about $80.

He left advertising in 2009 and has worked full-time as a photographer since.

## V. The household

He lives now in a small rented house outside Tsurui, in the central Kushiro wetlands.

With his wife Mari, a textile artist.

And a fifteen-year-old shiba inu named Kuro.

He spends roughly nine months of the year photographing.

Six of those nine in Hokkaido.

And a fifteen-year-old shiba inu named Kuro.

He spends roughly nine months of the year photographing.

Six of those nine in Hokkaido.

The other three on extended assignments that have, over the years, taken him to the Arctic National Wildlife Refuge, the Antarctic Peninsula, the South Georgia archipelago, Wrangel Island, and — for a single difficult expedition in 2018 — to the eastern edge of the Patagonian ice field.

## VI. What he does not do

He does not have a website.

He does not have an Instagram.

His agent, the small Tokyo-based agency Kogetsu, fields all inquiries.

He has refused, politely, every invitation to a workshop, a residency, or a judging panel that has come his way since approximately 2017.

He gives, on average, one interview per year.

## VII. Method

He gives, on average, one interview per year.

## VII. Method

His method is, by his own description, almost embarrassingly simple.

He scouts a location on foot, often for weeks before he attempts to photograph there.

He carries a single camera body.

A Nikon D850, which he has used since 2017 and refuses to replace.

Two lenses.

A 70-200mm f/2.8 and a 500mm f/4.

A small carbon tripod.

A thermos of barley tea.

He does not use camera traps.

He does not use drones.

He does not use blinds, except occasionally a piece of white cloth he drapes over himself for one or two hours at a time.

He does not bait.

He shoots, by his own count, between 200 and 400 frames per day.

## VIII. The famous frame

He does not bait.

He shoots, by his own count, between 200 and 400 frames per day.

## VIII. The famous frame

The famous *National Geographic* cover image was the 91st frame of a session that began at 3:47 p.m. and ended at 4:18 p.m.

On the evening of February 19, 2021.

In temperatures of approximately minus 18 Celsius.

With a wind off the Sea of Okhotsk that he later described as "the only condition under which a person can keep a 500mm lens absolutely still — when you cannot feel your hands at all, you also cannot tremble."

## IX. The work

Tanaka's published work to date consists of three monographs, all from the small Tokyo publisher Iwanami Shashin.

*Tancho* (2013), 64 plates.

*Ice and Wing* (2018), 88 plates, with text by the ornithologist Saito Kenji.

*Tancho* (2013), 64 plates.

*Ice and Wing* (2018), 88 plates, with text by the ornithologist Saito Kenji.

*The Long Wait* (2023), 112 plates, with an introduction by the writer Pico Iyer that has been widely anthologized.

He has been the subject of two *National Geographic* features (March 2019; January 2022).

A *New York Times* Sunday magazine profile (December 2020).

A 47-minute NHK documentary (2022) that follows him across a single winter and contains, in its first 12 minutes, almost no dialogue.

His prints, when they are made available, sell through a small Kyoto gallery called Kissa for between ¥800,000 and ¥3.2 million.

He releases editions of seven.

## X. On the question of identity

He releases editions of seven.

## X. On the question of identity

Because Tanaka shares his name with several other public figures, he is occasionally misidentified.

Most prominently with an arbitrator who works on patent disputes between American and Japanese companies.

And with a chef whose Berkeley restaurant has recently received considerable press.

He has been asked, in the past five years, whether he is a lawyer.

He is not.

Whether he has a doctorate.

He does not — he holds only a Bachelor of Fine Arts.

Whether he has any opinion at all about the international framework for standard-essential patents.

Whether he has any opinion at all about the international framework for standard-essential patents.

He laughed, when an interviewer asked him this in 2022, and said: "I have an opinion about the framework for standing in deep snow for nine hours, which is what I have spent the last twenty years learning. The other things are not my business."

## XI. The meeting

His agent has politely asked, on several occasions, that the photographer not be confused with the arbitrator.

The two have, in fact, met.

Once.

At a fundraiser at the Japan Society in San Francisco in 2019, where they were seated at adjoining tables.

The photographer reported that they spent five minutes establishing that they were unrelated.

The photographer reported that they spent five minutes establishing that they were unrelated.

And then ten minutes discussing red-crowned cranes, about which the arbitrator turned out to know a surprising amount.

## XII. On the future of the work

Tanaka has said, recently, that he is approaching the end of the crane project.

The book *The Long Wait* was conceived, he has said, as a kind of farewell.

Not to the birds, whose population is now relatively stable.

But to a particular Hokkaido winter that, as anyone who has photographed there for two decades will tell you, no longer exists.

The sea ice off Notsuke now arrives later and departs earlier than it did when he began.

The sea ice off Notsuke now arrives later and departs earlier than it did when he began.

The Kushiro wetlands, which froze reliably by mid-December in the early 2000s, sometimes do not freeze fully until late January now.

The cranes, which evolved for a specific kind of winter, are encountering a different one.

## XIII. The record

What this means for the photographs, he says, is that the photographs are no longer about the birds.

"They are about a season that is being lost, and a place that is changing under the birds' feet," he told NHK in 2022.

"The birds will adapt or they will not. The photographs are the record of what was."

## XIV. What is next

He has hinted at a next project.

He will not say what it is.

## XIV. What is next

He has hinted at a next project.

He will not say what it is.

His agent has confirmed that a fourth monograph is "in early development" and is "not about cranes."
